data.title: Oxley Holdings Buys Back 100,000 Shares
data.description:
Oxley Holdings bought back 100,000 shares in the open market on Thursday for SG$6,917, according to a same-day filing with the Singapore Exchange.

The company has been authorized to buy back around 211.5 million shares under its current mandate, and has so far bought back 4.3 million shares.

data.title: De Grey: Northern Star Takeover Scheme Meeting Scheduled for April 16
Thailand to import more US autos, LNG, electronics, finance minister says
data.description:
Thailand plans to import more U.S. autos, liquefied natural gas and electronics to try to reduce the trade gap with the United States, the finance minister said on Friday.

The Southeast Asian country wants to narrow the gap as much as possible by importing more U.S. goods, Finance Minister Pichai Chunhavajira told reporters. Thailand has earlier said it plans to import U.S. agricultural goods.

The commerce ministry has said Thailand had a trade surplus with the United States of $35.4 billion last year, while Washington has put its deficit with Thailand at $45.6 billion.

data.title: U.S. 10-Year Treasury Yield Slips Below 4%Market Talk
data.description:
0543 GMT - U.S. Treasurys continue to rally, with prices rising and yields falling as investors remain on the side of caution awaiting the economic impact of President Trump's tariffs. The 10-year Treasury yield, which fell below 4% overnight, last trades 4 basis points lower at 3.989%, according to Tradeweb. "The solid rally is an indication that investors are more worried about a recession in the U.S. economy relative to the rising inflation due to the tariffs," says Danske Bank Research chief analyst Stefan Mellin in a note. The 10-year yield fell below 4% for the first time since early October, according to Tradeweb data. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0537 GMT - Thailand's economy is likely to be dented, after Trump imposed a 36% tariff rate on the Southeast Asian country, UOB economists write in a research note. The U.S. remains Thailand's largest export market, accounting for around 18% of total exports last year, they note. Thailand is among the top 10 countries most impacted by the tariff policy, they say. The country is particularly exposed to the wider consequences of slower global trade and subdued global growth, due to its significant dependence on international trade, they add. UOB lowers its 2025 GDP growth forecast for Thailand to 2.0% from 2.9%. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0535 GMT - The yen strengthens against several G-10 and Asian currencies amid a risk-off sentiment in the wake of U.S. President Trump's tariff announcement. "In a global risk-off scenario, the Japanese yen and U.S. rates should continue to perform well," Grasshopper Asset Management's Daniel Tan says in commentary. Although "Trump's tariff policy has darkened the outlook for the global economy, Japan's domestic data have shown progress toward the BOJ's stable inflation target, heightening the possibility of a rate hike" at its April 30-May 1 meeting, the portfolio manager adds. USD/JPY falls 0.3% to 145.57 and AUD/JPY slips 1.7% to 90.90, while EUR/JPY edges 0.1% higher to 161.55. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0451 GMT - The Singapore dollar strengthens slightly against its U.S. counterpart in the Asian session. The USD haven status faces questions following President Trump's tariffs, DBS Group Research's Chang Wei Liang says in commentary. The USD didn't benefit at all from risk aversion, though other safe havens such as CHF and JPY did, the forex and credit strategist says. "The USD sell-off is partly due to the negative implications for U.S. growth of the high tariffs," he says. Another reason is that Trump's efforts to reduce the U.S. trade deficit via punitive tariffs could hurt the appeal of USD as a reserve currency, the strategist adds. USD/SGD is 0.1% lower at 1.3316. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0450 GMT - President Donald Trump's sharper-than-expected reciprocal tariffs prompt Citigroup to cut its 2025 growth forecast for South Korea to 0.8% from 1.0%. The negative impact of tougher U.S. duties on the manufacturing and trade sectors will also likely drive the Bank of Korea to revise down its earlier projection of 1.5% growth this year, Citi economists Jin-Wook Kim and Jiuk Choi say in a note. They now expect the BOK to cut the base rate by 25 bps each in April, July and October to support the economy, saying that it could prefer to act ahead of the presidential election likely in late May through early June. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0357 GMT - Philippines' March CPI reading, which is below the BSP's target range of between 2.0% and 4.0%, "seals the deal" for a rate cut later this month, Barclays economist Shreya Sodhani writes in a note. Headline inflation rose 1.8% on year, the lowest since May 2020, Sodhani notes. There have been two months of low inflation prints since the BSP's February meeting, she says. BSP governor has also noted that a cut is on the table for April, she adds. "We think U.S. tariffs will be growth-negative and deflationary, keeping BSP on an easing track," she says. Barclays expects another rate cut in 4Q and the risk of one more cut this year due to tariffs.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0340 GMT - South Korean's Constitutional Court ruling to oust impeached President Yoon and clear the way for electing a new leader may relieve market jitters, but uncertainty still lies ahead, ING senior economist Min Joo Kang writes in a note. "We expect the relief rally to be short-lived as political uncertainty and deep divisions surrounding the presidential election rattle sentiment and fresh waves of reciprocal [U.S.] tariffs hit the economy," Kang says. The Korean won gains strength against the U.S. dollar after the ruling, with USD/KRW 2.0% lower at 1,440.20 in Seoul onshore trading. The Bank of Korea may cut interest rates again in May to support the economy, Kang adds. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0303 GMT - The PBOC could return to easing monetary policy to support Chinese economy, given U.S. President Trump's tariffs are likely to drag China's GDP growth, DBS Group Research's Samuel Tse says in commentary. DBS expects 30 bps worth of cuts to the 1-year loan prime rate and 100 bps worth of cuts to the reserve requirement ratio this year, together with resumption of the government bond-buying program. Downside risks to economy could also be cushioned by stronger fiscal support and supply of Chinese government bonds, the senior economist says. The PBOC may tighten liquidity offshore to support China's exchange rate, Tse adds. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0209 GMT - The Monetary Authority of Singapore is likely to ease its monetary policy later this month, as risks shift from inflation to growth, Goldman Sachs economists say in a note. Although frontloading activities continued in 1Q, real GDP growth momentum could slow in 2Q due to direct U.S. tariffs, slower global growth and more global uncertainties, they write. They expect Singapore's real GDP growth to fall to 2.4% this year, compared to 2024's 4.0% expansion. Risks to Singapore's growth are skewed to the downside, especially from a further slowdown in global growth and global uncertainties, they say. With inflationary pressures easing significantly in January and February, they also expect headline and core CPI inflation to remain muted this year.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0157 GMT - Policy easing in Asia won't be enough to offset the hit to growth from sweeping Trump tariffs, Morgan Stanley analysts write in a note. The analysts expect policymakers in Asia to take up more fiscal and monetary easing in response to U.S. trade policy. However, the magnitude of easing in Asia may be more moderate during this cycle given more limited fiscal room this time around on account of higher public-debt-to-GDP ratios, MS say. Central banks in Asia will likely cut rates more in this cycle compared with the Trump 1.0 era between 2018-2019, MS says. That said, the pace of monetary easing could be constrained by the Fed's moves, the bank says. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0151 GMT - The dollar is expected to trade with a moderate upward bias versus Asian currencies on U.S. tariffs, though restrained by a relatively stable USD/CNY, Goldman Sachs's research team says in a report. GS notes the U.S. tariff announcements were more sweeping than expected. "Given the size (of) the tariffs, external environment and starting level, we think THB will be one of the most vulnerable currencies in Asia," the bank says. Also, "recent initiatives in Indonesia have raised investor concerns, and hence we think the IDR may continue to be under pressure," GS says. Other currency pairs such as USD/KRW, USD/TWD, USD/SGD and USD/MYR should also trade higher, it adds. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0149 GMT - China may accelerate consumption stimulus in coming months given the bigger-than-expected potential impact from U.S. tariffs, Citi analysts write in a note. Consumption stimulus would benefit China consumer discretionary players the most, they say. Investors are also increasingly preferring China-focused consumer stocks given geopolitical concerns and external uncertainty, they say. Citi is more positive on companies with low sales exposure to overseas markets, and the bank continues to be constructive on high-quality consumer discretionary companies. Anta, Haidilao, Chow Tai Fook Jewellery Group and Atour Lifestyle are the brokerage's top picks in the China consumer sector. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

data.title: Wanhua Chemical Group Achieves Production at Yantai Ethylene Project
data.description:
Wanhua Chemical Group on Thursday achieved production at the ethylene unit of Wanhua Chemical Yantai Industrial Park.

The 1.2 million-ton-per-year project produces ethylene products using naphtha and ethane as raw materials, according to a Friday filing with the Shanghai bourse.

data.title: Indonesia to Relax Trade Rules, Seek U.S. Talks Over 32% Tariff
data.description:
Indonesia has pledged to ease trade regulations as it prepares to negotiate with the U.S. over a planned 32% tariff on its exports.

President Prabowo Subianto has instructed his cabinet to simplify trade rules and loosen non-tariff barriers, the Coordinating Ministry of Economic Affairs said on April 3.

The government is assessing the potential impact of the tariffs, which are expected to hit key export sectors such as textiles, electronics, footwear, fishery products, and palm oil.

A delegation will be sent to Washington, D.C., to negotiate before the tariffs take effect on April 9.

Indonesia is also coordinating with Malaysia, the current ASEAN chair, to consider a joint response.

The ministry said easing trade rules is part of a broader strategy to demonstrate Jakarta's willingness to cooperate and reduce tensions.

Thailand is among the top 10 countries most impacted by the tariff policy, they say. The country is particularly exposed to the wider consequences of slower global trade and subdued global growth, due to its significant dependence on international trade, they add. data.title: Poland - Factors to Watch April 4
data.description:
The following are significant news stories, press reports and events on Friday. ALL TIMES GMT (Poland: GMT + 2 hour):

INTEREST RATES

Poland's central bank could ease monetary policy as soon as May with the cost of credit falling as low as 3.5% in 2026, Poland's central bank governor said on Thursday, as he shifted to a dovish stance that sent the zloty currency tumbling.

CENTRAL BANK

On Friday at 1200 the National Bank of Poland (NBP) will publish a minutes from the Monetary Policy Council (MPC) meeting in March.

POLISH BANKS

According to estimates by the Polish Bank Association, the involvement of banks in financing Poland's borrowing needs is already 690 billion zlotys ($180.84 billion). Data from the Ministry of Finance show that this constitutes 42% of the entire public debt, Rzeczpospolita daily reported.

DEBT

On Friday at 1000 the Ministry of Finance will hold an auction of 46-week treasury bills worth 3.0-6.0 billion zlotys($786.25 million- $ 1.57 billion).

LPP

The Polish clothing company LPP is in the process of comprehensively refinancing its working capital and investment needs. This amounts to several billion zlotys, of which 3-4 billion zlotys is the investment part, said LPP CEO and founder Marek Piechocki in an interview for Rzeczpospolita daily.

****Reuters has not verified stories reported by Polish media and does not vouch for their accuracy.****

data.title: Market Chatter: Philippines DOF Warns of US Tariff Impact
data.description:
The Philippines' Department of Finance warned a 17% reciprocal tariff from the US could disrupt supply chains, fuel inflation, and pressure the peso, BusinessMirror reported Friday.

Despite this, exporters see an opportunity, as the rate is lower than tariffs imposed on other Asian countries like China and Vietnam. Finance Secretary Ralph Recto believes the domestic demand-driven economy offers some buffer against global trade shocks, reportedly.

Meanwhile, industry groups say the tariff could attract investors shifting production to the Philippines to avoid higher duties elsewhere. Key export products affected include electronics, automotive parts, and leather goods.

The US remained the Philippines' top export market in 2024, accounting for 16.6% of total exports, the report added.

data.title: India Stocks Head for Steep Losses Weekly
data.description:
India's equities slumped 740 points, or 1.0%, to 75,555 in early deals on Friday, marking a decline for the second straight session as most sectors retreated, hitting their lowest level in two weeks.

The BSE Sensex tracked a plunge on Wall Street overnight amid worries about a global trade war, raising concerns over a potential recession.

The pharmaceutical sector plummeted 6.1%, followed by metals (-5.2%) and autos (-3.0%), ahead of new tariffs set to take effect on April 5.

Capping the fall, the final survey showed India's private sector growth was revised higher in March, marking the highest level in seven months.

The index is set to close the week on a low note following rises in the prior two weeks, plunging around 2.4% so far, amid recession fears triggered by the global trade war.

The Nifty 50 dropped 1.2% to below 23,000, with notable losses from Tata Steel (-6.1%), Tata Motors (-5.4%), Sun Pharmaceutical (-4.6%), Larsen & Toubro (-4.2%), and IndusInd Bank (-3.8%).

data.title: Premier Energies Forms Subsidiary for Aluminum Manufacturing
data.description:
Integrated solar cell and solar module manufacturing company Premier Energies has incorporated a wholly-owned subsidiary in India named Premier-Green Aluminium, according to a Thursday filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

The new entity will deal in manufacturing, processing, and extruding aluminum and its alloys into various products for captive consumption.

data.title: Cicor Technologies to Buy Spanish Electronics Manufacturer MADES
data.description:
Cicor Technologies agreed to purchase Malaga Aerospace, Defense & Electronics Systems, or MADES, a manufacturer of electronics for the aerospace and defense industry based in Spain.

The deal will be financed from current credit lines and is anticipated to be closed in the second half, pending regulatory approvals, the Swiss electronic components company said Friday.

data.title: Maker of Safety Gloves Says It Will Raise Prices in U.S.
data.description:
By Stuart Condie

Many companies are taking a wait-and-see attitude on responding to President Trump's tariffs, but one company has made up its mind. Australia's Ansell, which makes safety gloves for industrial and medical uses, said Friday it would offset the effects of tariffs by charging customers more.

Ansell generates about 43% of its revenue in the U.S. and makes most of its products in Asian countries that face tariffs of more than 20% and sometimes above 40%. "U.S. industry manufacturing capacity for comparable hand and body protection products is negligible," Ansell said in a market filing, explaining why it is choosing to keep manufacturing in Asia.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: What you need to know about investigations faced by South Korea's former president Yoon
data.description:
Since South Korea's Constitutional Court ousted President Yoon Suk Yeol on Friday, he has lost presidential immunity and is vulnerable to additional charges on top of an ongoing criminal trial concerning his martial law declaration on Dec. 3.

Yoon is currently undergoing a trial for insurrection charges, along with a former cabinet minister, several military commanders and police officials.

Here is what we know about his criminal investigations and trial so far:

WHO IS FACING CHARGES?

Yoon has been charged with masterminding insurrection over his martial law order. His former defence minister Kim Yong-hyun has been arrested and indicted for alleged insurrection and abuse of power.

Chiefs of the Capital Defence Command and the Defence Counterintelligence Command have also been indicted.

Others involved in the case include former Interior Minister Lee Sang-min and army chief Park An-su, who was named martial law commander. The army's Special Warfare Command as well as several other military generals and senior police officials, including the national and Seoul police chiefs, are also accused of playing a role in the martial law plan.

Yoon, Kim, Lee, the national and Seoul police chiefs and more than 10 military commanders have been banned from leaving the country.

Yoon became the first sitting president to be arrested on January 15 after a dramatic showdown with law enforcement officials.

He was released from jail on March 8 after a court cancelled his arrest warrant, citing the timing of the indictment and questions about the legality of the investigation process.

WHAT CHARGES ARE SOUGHT?

In what had been the first-ever criminal prosecution of an incumbent leader, Yoon was indicted in January on charges of leading insurrection.

Other officials also face charges of insurrection, abuse of authority and obstructing other people from exercising their rights.

South Korean investigating authorities pursued the charge against Yoon because insurrection is one of the few criminal charges from which a sitting South Korean president does not have immunity.

However, with former president Yoon having lost immunity, he is now open to other potential charges prosecutors may bring against him.

If convicted, leading an insurrection is punishable by death or life imprisonment with or without prison labour.

For those who engaged in activities key to the insurrection, punishment could range from death to life imprisonment down to imprisonment without prison labour for at least five years.

South Korea last handed down a death sentence in 2016, but has not executed anyone since 1997.

People who merely joined in the plot or violence face imprisonment, with or without prison labour, for less than five years.

In February, Yoon attended in a Seoul court for his first criminal trial hearing, where his lawyers denied insurrection charges and said Yoon had no intention to paralyse the country.

The trial's first arguments are scheduled on April 14.

data.title: CapMan Closes New Fund at $120 Million
data.description:
Private equity investor CapMan said Friday it raised $120 million in new capital through CapMan Wealth's annual program.

CW Investment Partners Fund IV held its final close at the end of March. It invests alongside AlpInvest under the annual CapMan Wealth Investment Partners program launched in 2021.

data.title: R&S Group Opens New Transformer Plant in Poland
data.description:
R&S Group inaugurated a new greenfield oil distribution transformer facility in Krzeczów, Poland, on Thursday, according to a Friday release.

The plant operating under the Swiss electrical infrastructure products manufacturer's Rauscher & Stoecklin brand is a production center for its expansion into markets like the Baltics, the Nordics and Germany. It is expected to increase the company's production capacity, with a target of over 1,000 units in 2025.

data.title: 17LIVE Group Buys Back More Shares
data.description:
17LIVE Group repurchased 50,000 shares worth SG$0.78 apiece on Thursday, according to a same-day filing on the Singapore Exchange.

The social entertainment platform has been mandated to buy back around 17.7 million shares. To date, it has bought back 905,500 shares.

data.title: Australian Rare Earths Says Assays Confirm Near-Surface Mineralization at South Australia Uranium Project
data.description:
Australian Rare Earths said assays confirm uranium intersections in near-surface mineralization at its Overland uranium project in South Australia, according to a Friday Australian bourse filing.

Intersections from the drilling came in at two meters at 92 parts-per-million of triuranium octoxide from 26 meters and one meter at 72 ppm of triuranium octoxide from 31 meters.

The program aims to follow up on the shallow uranium occurrence

intersected in the target one area to determine the extent of the mineralization, and to test target two for similar mineralization style.

data.title: Shell to Sell Its Interest in Colonial Enterprises to Brookfield for $1.45 Billion
data.description:
By Adam Whittaker

Shell said it would sell its 16.125% stake in Colonial Enterprises to Brookfield Infrastructure Partners for $1.45 billion as it continues to simplify is portfolio.

The British energy major said the sale is subject to regulatory approvals but is expected to complete in the fourth quarter of the year.

Shell said the divestment continues to simplify its portfolio and would enable it to refocus on areas where it has scale and competitive advantage.

Colonial Enterprises operates and fully owns Colonial Pipeline Company ,which provides oil products transportation services from the U.S. Gulf Coast to the U.S. Atlantic Seaboard. It also owns the Colonial Marketing Company.

The stake was sold to Colossus AcquireCo, which is a subsidiary of Brookfield Infrastructure Partners.

Write to Adam Whittaker at adam.whittaker@wsj.com

data.title: Indonesia Prepares Strategic Response to U.S. Tariff, Plans to Seek Negotiations
data.description:
By Ying Xian Wong

Indonesia is preparing strategic measures and intensifying diplomatic efforts in response to the U.S.'s decision to impose a 32% reciprocal tariff on Indonesian exports, a senior official has said.

The government plans to send a high-level delegation to Washington to negotiate directly with U.S. officials, said Susiwijono Moegiarso, secretary of the Coordinating Ministry for Economic Affairs said in a statement Thursday.

That comes after the President Trump announced a 32% tariff on Indonesia under a new reciprocal tariff regime that has targeted dozens of countries. The new headwind arrives at a delicate time for the Southeast Asian economy, where government policy shifts have caused concerns about its financial stability, leading to a sharp selloff in equities and a slump in the rupiah.

Indonesia's government is working with Malaysia, which holds the chairmanship of the Association of Southeast Asian Nations, to coordinate a regional response as all member countries have been affected by the U.S.'s reciprocal tariff plan, Moegiarso added.

Vietnam, Thailand and Malaysia face some of the toughest tariffs at 46%, 36%, and 24%, respectively.

The new duty would significantly hurt the competitiveness of Indonesia's exports to the U.S., particularly in key sectors such as electronics, textiles, footwear, palm oil, and rubber, Moegiarso said.

Jakarta plans to assess the impact the barriers will have on these sectors and the Indonesian economy, while implementing measures to mitigate negative effects, he added.

Indonesian President Prabowo Subianto is also pursuing structural reforms, including deregulation efforts aimed at improving competitiveness and attracting investment, according to the statement.

The government is working with the central bank to stabilize the rupiah and ensure foreign-currency liquidity to support businesses and maintain stability, he said.

Write to Ying Xian Wong at yingxian.wong@wsj.com

data.title: Uruguay Approves Dabeinong's Pest, Weed-Resistant Soybean Variants
data.description:
Authorities in Uruguay approved Beijing Dabeinong Technology's insect-resistant and herbicide-resistant soybean variants, according to a Friday filing with the Shenzhen bourse.

The DBN9004 herbicide-resistant transgenic soybean is able to tolerate glyphosate and glufosinate-based herbicides, helping to eliminate weeds in South America, the filing said.

Both Argentina and Brazil approved the planting of the soybean variant in 2019 and 2023, respectively, according to Dabeinong.

The DBN8002 variant is resistant to glufosinate herbicides and can resist up to four major pests such as fall armyworm and beet armyworm in China and 10 major pests in the Americas, the filing said.

Uruguay also approved a cross between the DBN9004 and the DBN8002 variants.
